questions.question: Why was Akbar's position unstable and insecure when he ascended the throne of Delhi?
questions.answer:
Akbar's position was unstable and insecure when he ascended the throne for the following reasons:

1. He was only 13 years old when he became emperor, making him too young to rule effectively on his own.
2. Adil Shah, the nephew of Sher Shah, was determined to capture Delhi and re-establish Afghan rule over India.
3. Hemu, the chief minister and general of Adil Shah, had already occupied Delhi and Agra, posing a direct military threat.
4. The Rajputs had become powerful and were waiting for an opportunity to expel the Mughals from India.
5. Kabul was occupied by Akbar's half-brother, who was ruling independently and refused to acknowledge Akbar's authority.

questions.question: Discuss the subjugation of Rana Udai Singh and the conflict with Rana Pratap Singh.
questions.answer:
(a) Subjugation of Rana Udai Singh:
Rana Udai Singh was the head of the Rajput clans and refused to submit to Akbar. He also rejected the offer of a matrimonial alliance. Akbar marched against Chittor to subdue him. Udai Singh fled to the hills, leaving Jai Mal to command the defence of the fort. After a siege of four months, the resistance collapsed when Jai Mal was killed. The Rajput women performed jauhar (self-immolation) rather than face capture. Chittor fell, followed by Ranthambore. By 1570, most Rajput princes had accepted Akbar as their overlord.

(b) Conflict with Rana Pratap Singh:
After Udai Singh built the city of Udaipur and died, his son Rana Pratap Singh continued the struggle against the Mughals. The Battle of Haldighati was fought in 1576, where the Mughal forces were led by Raja Man Singh. Rana Pratap was defeated but managed to escape. He continued his resistance from the hills and recovered parts of his lost territory, but he could never recapture Chittor.

questions.question: Discuss the power and position of the emperor and his council of ministers in the central administration.
questions.answer:
Power and Position of the Emperor:
The emperor had supreme authority in all military, political, administrative and judicial matters. His word was law. He was the commander-in-chief of the armed forces and the supreme judge of the empire. The appointment, promotion and dismissal of all officials was the emperor's sole right.

Council of Ministers:
The emperor was assisted by a council of ministers:
1. Wazir or Diwan - Responsible for revenue and finance of the empire.
2. Mir Bakshi - In charge of military administration and acted as paymaster of the armed forces.
3. Chief Sadr or Qazi - The chief judge who administered justice according to law.
4. Mir Saman - In charge of the royal household and its management.
5. Vakil - Originally the Prime Minister, but under Akbar this post was reduced to a ceremonial role.

All ministers were appointed, promoted and dismissed solely by the emperor, ensuring that supreme power remained with the ruler.

questions.question: Explain five main features of the mansabdari system introduced by Akbar.
questions.answer:
The main features of the mansabdari system introduced by Akbar were:

1. Every official in the Mughal administration was given a rank or mansab. The lowest rank was that of a commander of 10 horsemen, and the highest rank was that of a commander of 7000 horsemen.
2. All mansabdars were appointed, promoted and dismissed solely by the emperor, ensuring direct imperial control over the administration.
3. Mansabdars were paid salaries according to their rank. Later, instead of cash salaries, they were given jagirs (land grants) from which they could collect revenue.
4. The mansab was NOT hereditary. When a mansabdar died, his rank and jagir reverted to the king. This prevented the growth of a feudal class that could challenge the emperor.
5. Mansabdars had no authority over the administration of their jagirs. They could only collect the revenue assigned to them.
6. To prevent fraud, the branding of horses and the maintenance of descriptive rolls of soldiers were adopted as measures of verification.

questions.question: Explain the important features of Todar Mal's land revenue system (Bandobast).
questions.answer:
The important features of Todar Mal's land revenue system were:

1. All land was carefully surveyed and measured using a bamboo jarib (measuring rod) joined by iron rings, which was more accurate than the hemp rope used earlier.
2. The land was grouped into four categories based on whether it was under regular or occasional cultivation.
3. Each category of land was further classified as good, middling or bad based on the quality of the soil.
4. The average produce and average local prices over the last 10 years were calculated for each category of land.
5. One-third of the average produce was fixed as the king's share (revenue demand).
6. Revenue could be paid in cash or kind, though cash payment was preferred by the government.
7. Revenue officers were strictly instructed to treat the peasants well and not to use harsh methods of collection.
8. Loans were advanced to peasants for purchasing seeds and cattle when needed.
9. Remission of revenue was granted if crops failed due to droughts, floods or other natural calamities.

questions.question: What policies did Akbar follow to transform the Islamic state into a secular one?
questions.answer:
Akbar followed several policies to transform the Islamic state into a secular one:

1. He abolished the jizya tax, which was a tax imposed only on non-Muslims and was deeply resented by the Hindus.
2. He abolished the pilgrim tax that was imposed on Hindu pilgrims visiting holy places.
3. Hindus were allowed to build temples, celebrate their festivals and worship freely without any restrictions.
4. Akbar married Rajput princesses and allowed them complete freedom of worship. They were permitted to practise their religion within the royal palace.
5. He gave high posts and positions of responsibility to Hindus, such as Todar Mal (revenue minister), Birbal (advisor), and Raja Bhagwan Das (military commander).
6. He gave land grants to people of all religions, including Hindus, Jains and Christians, for building places of worship.
7. He built the Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) at Fatehpur Sikri where scholars from all religions could come together for inter-faith discussions and debates.
8. He issued the Mahzar (decree of infallibility) in 1579, which gave him the authority to settle religious disputes, reducing the power of orthodox Muslim clergy.

questions.question: Briefly explain the features and objectives of the Din-i-Ilahi.
questions.answer:
The Din-i-Ilahi was a new religious order announced by Akbar in 1582. Its main features and objectives were:

Objectives:
1. To establish a religious order that would be acceptable to people of all faiths.
2. To promote universal brotherhood and national unity among the diverse religious communities of the empire.

Features:
1. It was a combination of the religious ideas and principles of major religions such as Islam, Hinduism, Christianity, Zoroastrianism and Jainism.
2. It was based on the belief in one God, with the emperor as God's representative on earth.
3. It was more of a moral code of conduct rather than an organized religion.
4. It had no sacred books, no priestly classes, no places of worship and no elaborate rituals.
5. Akbar never forced conversion to the Din-i-Ilahi on anyone.

Failure:
The Din-i-Ilahi was not popular outside the court. Most courtiers refused to accept it, including Raja Man Singh and Raja Bhagwan Das. Birbal was the only Hindu to accept the new faith. The Din-i-Ilahi came to an end with the death of Akbar.

questions.question: Give an account of Akbar's cultural achievements with reference to Literature and Architecture.
questions.answer:
Akbar made significant cultural contributions in the fields of Literature and Architecture:

Literature:
1. Persian was the official court language during Akbar's reign.
2. Faizi was one of the greatest Persian poets in Akbar's court.
3. Abul Fazl, a renowned scholar, wrote the Akbarnama, which is a detailed chronicle of Akbar's reign.
4. Birbal was honoured as the Hindi Kavi Raj (poet laureate) for his literary contributions.
5. Tulsidas, though not a courtier, wrote the famous Ramacharitamanas during Akbar's reign.
6. Although Akbar himself was illiterate, he was a great patron of learning and maintained a library of 24,000 books.
7. He established a translation department that translated important works from Sanskrit, Arabic and Greek into Persian, promoting cross-cultural learning.

Architecture:
1. Initially, Akbar's buildings followed the Persian style, as seen in Humayun's Tomb in Delhi.
2. Later, he developed a unique style that blended Indian and Rajput architectural elements.
3. Fatehpur Sikri, described as a 'dream in stone', was built entirely in red sandstone. It includes magnificent structures such as the Diwan-i-Khas (Hall of Private Audience), Panch Mahal (five-storeyed palace), and Jodha Bai's Palace.
4. The Buland Darwaza (Gate of Magnificence) at Fatehpur Sikri is one of the tallest gateways in the world.
5. The Agra Fort was rebuilt in red sandstone and served as the main seat of Mughal power.
